summary: Hardcoded WhatsApp pairing code in Baileys fork enables account-linking hijack
details: This package presents itself as a drop-in fork of the Baileys WhatsApp library (scoped name @blckrose/baileys, README and exports mirror the upstream API: makeWASocket, useMultiFileAuthState, requestPairingCode, etc.). In lib/Socket/socket.js around lines 597-601, the requestPairingCode(phoneNumber, customPairingCode) implementation replaces the upstream library's randomly-generated pairing code with a fixed constant 'BLCKRO53' when the caller does not supply a customPairingCode. The constant is encoded as a String.fromCharCode array ([66,76,67,75,82,79,53,51]) to hide it from casual review, and a comment above acknowledges it as a 'Custom fixed pairing code: BLCKROSE'. The package banner in lib/index.js even prints the constant on startup. The README documents the call as `await sock.requestPairingCode(number)` with no second argument — so the default usage is the vulnerable path. Any installer following the documented usage exposes the WhatsApp account-linking ceremony to anyone who knows the constant (which is publicly visible in the package): an attacker who races the user can complete the pairing using this known code and link the victim's WhatsApp account to an attacker-controlled device. The pairing constant is hardcoded (not configurable per-deployment), so all installers share the same predictable secret.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
